You see a claim that "the average return" of an investment fund is a certain percentage. How likely are you to check whether that average is skewed by a few outlier years before trusting it?
A campaign shares a statistic about crime rates dropping under a certain policy. How likely are you to check whether the comparison period was chosen to make the numbers look better than they are?
A neighborhood group chat shares a statistic about local crime increasing. How likely are you to repeat the statistic to others without checking where it came from?
An article claims a new study "proves" a link between screen time and a health outcome. How likely are you to check whether the study only shows correlation before repeating the claim as proven fact?
A review site shows a product with a 4.9 star rating. How likely are you to check the number of reviews behind that rating before trusting it?
Two different outlets report different numbers for the same event. How likely are you to look up the original source of the data before deciding which number to trust?
A slide in a presentation shows a steep upward trend line. How likely are you to accept the trend as meaningful without checking what time range the data actually covers?
An influencer claims their method "worked for 95% of people." How likely are you to ask how that percentage was measured before trying the method yourself?

A wellness account posts a "before and after" comparison to promote a product. How likely are you to consider other factors, like diet, exercise, or time, that could explain the change before crediting the product?
A study cited in a lecture reports a striking result from a very small sample. How likely are you to treat the result as solid evidence without noting the small sample size?
A budgeting app shows you a chart claiming users save a certain amount per month. How likely are you to check how that average was calculated before trusting the number?
You see a claim that a certain policy caused unemployment to drop, based on a short time window. How likely are you to check a longer time period to see if the trend actually holds?
A relative shares a statistic from a forwarded message about a health risk. How likely are you to verify the number against a credible source before believing it?
A tech blog claims a new AI model is "twice as smart" based on one benchmark test. How likely are you to accept that claim without checking what the benchmark actually measures?
An ad claims "customers save an average of $200." How likely are you to ask how that average was calculated, and whether outliers skew it, before trusting the claim?
A chart in a news story shows two lines that appear to move together over time. How likely are you to consider that a third factor might explain both trends before assuming one caused the other?

A survey of your company's employees shows high satisfaction, but only 15% of staff responded. How likely are you to factor in the low response rate before trusting that the results represent everyone?
A meme presents a statistic with no source cited. How likely are you to share it anyway because it feels true?
A friend says a diet trend is proven effective because a celebrity lost weight on it. How likely are you to point out that one example isn't the same as solid evidence?
A research paper you're citing reports a result with a very wide margin of error. How likely are you to take that margin of error into account before stating the finding as fact?
An investment ad shows a chart of dramatic gains over a short, cherry-picked window. How likely are you to be persuaded by the chart without checking performance over a longer period?

A pundit claims a survey proves most people support a policy, but the survey only polled people in one narrow group. How likely are you to question whether that sample represents the wider population?
You hear that a local restaurant's rating dropped sharply. How likely are you to check how many new reviews came in before assuming the quality actually declined?
A press release claims a new drug is "significantly more effective," without giving numbers. How likely are you to look for the actual data before accepting the claim?
A site shows "only 2 left in stock" and a claimed discount percentage. How likely are you to take the discount percentage at face value without checking the item's price history?
A poll result gets reported with a small margin of error. How likely are you to check what that margin of error actually means before treating small differences between numbers as meaningful?
Your manager credits a new training program for a rise in productivity numbers. How likely are you to ask whether the busy season or other factors could explain the rise instead?
A post shows two graphs side by side using different scales to make a comparison look dramatic. How likely are you to notice the mismatched scales before accepting the comparison?
A supplement label cites "clinical studies" without naming them. How likely are you to trust the claim anyway because it sounds scientific?
You're comparing two schools using a ranking chart. How likely are you to check what factors the ranking is actually based on before trusting it?
A finance influencer claims a strategy "beats the market," using one good year as proof. How likely are you to ask about its performance across multiple years before trusting the claim?

A news graphic shows public opinion "shifting dramatically" using a bar chart with a narrow y-axis range. How likely are you to check the actual percentage-point difference before accepting it as dramatic?
A friend claims that most people they know agree with them on an issue, as proof they're right. How likely are you to point out that a small circle of friends isn't a representative sample?
An article says a new study "links" a common habit to a health outcome. How likely are you to check whether the study actually shows causation, not just a correlation, before repeating the claim?
You see a graph on a product page with no labeled units on one axis. How likely are you to notice this missing detail before trusting the chart's message?
